behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops
behaviors.justification: This is pure algorithmic logic with no I/O or external dependencies. The test provides pre-calculated coordinate sets (no geocoding API needed) and compares heuristic output against known-optimal solutions (either pre-computed via reference solvers or hardcoded test fixtures). Distance calculations happen entirely in-process. No mocking can hide an algorithmic failure to meet the 1.5x bound — the comparison is deterministic.
behaviors.test_description: Create unit tests with 5, 10, 15, and 20-stop coordinate sets. For each, either pre-compute the optimal route length (using a reference solver or manual calculation for small instances) or use known test cases. Pass coordinates to the heuristic, calculate resulting route length, and assert route_length ≤ 1.5 * optimal_length. Include test cases covering: dense clusters, linear layouts, scattered points. Edge cases: 2-3 stops (trivial), all stops at same location.

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type
behaviors.justification: Time calculation is deterministic math: time = distance / speed. No I/O, no external calls. Configuration (speed per road type) can be injected via constructor or test doubles. All inputs and outputs are testable in-process. No mock can hide a miscalculation in the formula itself.
behaviors.test_description: Create unit tests that inject pre-calculated distances and configured speeds (including multiple road types: highway, urban, rural). For each combination, assert computed_time = distance / speed. Test edge cases: zero distance (should yield zero time), very long distances, boundary speeds, mismatched road types. Test that different road types use their configured speeds correctly. Verify floating-point handling (e.g., 100 meters at 50 km/h should compute to 7.2 seconds).

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount
behaviors.justification: Pure validation logic: comparison and arithmetic. Given total_time and shift_remaining, the validator either accepts or rejects and computes excess. All inputs are deterministic, all outputs are verifiable in-process. No external calls, no mocks that could hide the core logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create unit tests with scenarios: (1) total_time < shift_remaining (should accept), (2) total_time == shift_remaining (clarify spec: accept or reject?), (3) total_time > shift_remaining (should reject, excess = total_time - shift_remaining). Test edge cases: zero excess (just over limit), large excess, negative shift_remaining (should reject), floating-point precision near boundaries. Verify excess amount is computed and returned accurately.

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on realistic error-handling across the geocoding API client boundary. While a simple unit test with a trivial mock (always returning 'no results') could verify basic skip/report logic, it would miss real failure modes: timeout handling, malformed responses, rate-limiting, partial results, or cascading failures when multiple addresses fail. The service must coordinate between error handling, route re-optimization, and reporting—these cross multiple logical boundaries. A unit test cannot reliably simulate the variety of real API error states without sophisticated mocking; integration-level testing with a realistic mock (or test double) of the API client is needed to catch subtle failures in error propagation and recovery.
behaviors.test_description: Create integration tests that mock the geocoding API client to return no results for specific test addresses and success for others. Verify: (1) service skips failed address from the route, (2) remaining addresses are optimized normally, (3) output includes a list of skipped addresses with reasons. Test additional failure scenarios: API timeout (mock slow client), HTTP error responses (500, 429 rate-limit), malformed JSON response, partial success (some addresses succeed, some fail). For each scenario, verify the service completes the route (with remaining addresses) rather than crashing or returning incomplete data.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice
behaviors.justification: Deduplication and merging are pure logic with no I/O. The service takes a list of addresses (strings or normalized objects) and combines items for duplicates. Equality check and item combination are deterministic operations. No external calls or mocks that could hide a deduplication failure.
behaviors.test_description: Create unit tests with input lists containing: (1) exact duplicate addresses (same string), (2) normalized duplicates (different spacing/casing, if applicable), (3) no duplicates, (4) all duplicates. For each, verify: (a) each unique address appears exactly once in the optimized route, (b) delivery items are merged correctly (if addresses have item counts, totals must match), (c) route optimization only visits unique addresses. Test edge cases: null items, empty item lists, single-item lists, large duplicate counts.
